title: How to Use Intuition When Making Life Decisions
meta_description: Stop making decisions with only half the data. Learn how to use your intuition as a real information stream for life choices that feel right in your body, not just on paper.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Life Decisions
og_description: Stop making decisions with only half the data. Learn how to use your intuition as a real information stream for life choices that feel right in your body, not just on paper.
body:
You've Been Making Decisions with Half the Information

Let's be real for a second. You've probably made some pretty big life decisions using only your brain. You weighed pros and cons. You made spreadsheets. You asked everyone you know for advice. And then you still ended up in a situation that felt... wrong. Not bad, necessarily. Just wrong. Like you were wearing someone else's coat.

That's not a failure of logic. That's a failure of access. You left out half the data.

Your intuition isn't some woo-woo magic trick reserved for psychics on TV. It's a real, measurable information stream your nervous system picks up long before your conscious mind catches on. And when you learn to use it for life decisions, you stop second-guessing yourself. You stop making choices that look good on paper but feel terrible in your body.

I'm Kalesh. I teach people how to work with their sensitivity instead of fighting it. And I'm telling you straight: you can trust your gut. You just need to know how to tell the difference between fear, wishful thinking, and actual intuitive information.

Right?! Let's get into it.

Why Your Brain Alone Will Never Be Enough

Your conscious mind processes about 40 bits of information per second. Your subconscious mind processes about 11 million bits per second. That's not a typo. Eleven million. Your brain is a bottleneck. It can only handle a tiny fraction of what your entire system is picking up.

So when you try to make a big decision using only logic, you're working with a fraction of the data. You're ignoring the 10,999,960 bits your body, your energy field, and your intuition are already processing. Does that land? Because that's why you can walk into a room and instantly feel something is off before anyone says a word. That's why you get a weird feeling about a job offer that looks perfect on paper. That's your full system trying to tell you something your brain can't articulate yet.

Your intuition is the translator between that massive subconscious data stream and your conscious awareness. It's not a guess. It's a summary.

"Intuition is not a feeling of certainty. It's a feeling of alignment. Certainty comes from the ego. Alignment comes from something deeper. Learn to tell the difference, and you'll never make a decision that betrays you again."

The Three Flavors of Intuitive Information

Before you can use intuition for decisions, you need to know what it feels like in your body. Most people think intuition is a loud, clear voice saying "do this" or "don't do that." It's not. Not usually. Intuitive information comes in three main flavors, and you've probably experienced all of them without knowing what they were.

1. The Body Signal

This is the most reliable and most ignored. Your body tells you the truth before your mind can spin a story. When you think about a decision, what happens in your body? Does your chest tighten? Do your shoulders relax? Does your stomach drop or settle? Does your throat feel tight or open?

Your body is a lie detector. It doesn't care about your plans, your parents' expectations, or what looks good on Instagram. It responds to truth. The trick is to slow down enough to feel it. Next time you're facing a choice, close your eyes and imagine choosing option A. Scan your body. Then imagine option B. Scan again. The one that makes your body feel more open, more settled, more alive - that's your intuitive signal.

2. The Knowing Without Knowing

This is the classic "I just know." You can't explain it. You don't have evidence. But you know. This is your subconscious handing you the conclusion without showing you the work. It's frustrating because you can't defend it in an argument. But it's often the most accurate.

If you've ever said "I had a bad feeling about that person" and later found out they were lying, or "I just knew I should take that job" and it changed your life, you've experienced this. The key is learning to trust it even when it doesn't make logical sense. That's hard. But it gets easier with practice.

3. The Synchronistic Nudge

Sometimes intuition doesn't come from inside you at all. It comes from the world around you. You think about someone and they call. You're stuck on a decision and you see a sign, literally. You overhear a conversation that answers your question. You find a book that falls open to the exact page you needed.

These aren't coincidences. They're your environment mirroring back what you already know but haven't accepted yet. When you start paying attention, these nudges become impossible to ignore. And they're incredibly useful for decisions.

How to Practice Before the Big Decision

You wouldn't run a marathon without training. Don't try to use intuition for a life-changing decision without practicing first. Start small. Like, embarrassingly small.

Practice on what to eat for lunch. Before you open the menu, check in with your body. What sounds good? Not what you "should" eat, not what's healthiest, not what your friend wants. What does your body want? Then order that. See how it feels.

Practice on which route to take home. When you hit an intersection, pause and sense which direction pulls you. Take it. Even if it's longer. Notice what happens.

Practice on who to call back first. When you have two messages, which one do you feel drawn to? Call that person. See what unfolds.

These tiny decisions build the muscle. They teach you the difference between intuition and impulse. Impulse is urgent and demanding. Intuition is quiet and patient. Impulse says "right now!" Intuition says "this way."

The Decision Protocol: A Step-by-Step Guide

Okay, now you're ready for the real thing. Here's a protocol you can use for any major life decision. Moving cities. Changing careers. Starting or ending a relationship. Making a big financial move. Use this every time.

Step 1: Get Still and Get Grounded

You can't hear intuition through noise. Turn off your phone. Close your laptop. Sit somewhere comfortable. Take three deep breaths. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel your body in space. You're not trying to clear your mind. You're trying to get present enough to feel what's already there.

This takes five minutes. If you can't do that, you're not ready to make a decision.

Step 2: State the Decision Clearly

Say it out loud. "I am deciding whether to take the job in Chicago." Or "I am deciding whether to end this relationship." Be specific. Vague questions get vague answers. Your intuition needs a clear target.

Step 3: Feel Both Options in Your Body

This is the core of the protocol. Imagine option A as vividly as you can. See yourself living that choice. Feel it in your body for 30 seconds. Notice everything - your chest, your stomach, your throat, your shoulders. Then let it go. Shake it off. Now imagine option B. Same thing. 30 seconds. Full body scan.

Compare the two body experiences. One probably felt more open, lighter, or more alive. The other probably felt tighter, heavier, or more closed. That's your answer. Not the one that "makes more sense." The one that makes your body feel more like itself.

Step 4: Ask One Question

Here's the question that cuts through all the noise: "If I knew I couldn't fail, which would I choose?" Your ego is scared of failure. Your intuition doesn't care. When you remove the fear of failure, what's left is truth.

Step 5: Wait 24 Hours

Intuition doesn't expire, but urgency does. If you're feeling pressure to decide right now, that's not intuition. That's fear or external pressure. Give yourself 24 hours. Sleep on it. Let your subconscious process. In the morning, check in again. The answer will be clearer.

What If You Get It Wrong?

This is the question everyone asks. What if I trust my intuition and it's wrong? Here's the thing: you've been getting it wrong using logic too. You just didn't call it wrong. You called it "learning" or "a mistake" or "I should have known better."

Intuition isn't infallible. It's a skill, not a superpower. You'll get it wrong sometimes. But you'll also get it right more often than you do now. And when you get it wrong, you'll learn to read the signal better. That's how every skill works.

The real risk isn't getting it wrong. The real risk is never learning to trust yourself. That's a life of outsourcing your decisions to other people, to logic, to fear. That's a life of looking back and wondering what could have been.

Tools to Support Your Practice

You don't need fancy tools to develop intuition. But the right ones can help. Here are a few that my students have found useful.

First, a good journal. Writing down your intuitive hits and misses helps you see patterns. You'll start to notice that your body signals are different from your emotional reactions. You'll see which decisions worked out and which didn't. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one has prompts that help you track your intuitive experiences.

Second, a book that explains the science behind intuition. It helps to know you're not making this up. There's real research showing that gut feelings are faster and often more accurate than conscious reasoning. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one breaks down how to make better decisions by combining intuition and analysis.

Third, a practice tool. Some people need a physical object to focus their attention. A stone, a crystal, a piece of jewelry. Something you can hold while you check in with your body. It creates a ritual, and ritual quiets the mind. See on Amazon (paid link) - this is a great guide for building your own intuitive practice.

Fourth, something for the skeptics in your life. Because not everyone will understand. When you start making decisions based on "a feeling," people will question you. Having a resource that explains the logic behind intuition can help you articulate what you're doing. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one is perfect for explaining intuition to the logical thinkers in your world.

The Fear That Pretends to Be Intuition

Here's the hardest part of using intuition for decisions. Fear and intuition feel very similar in the body. Both can make your stomach clench. Both can make your heart race. Both can feel urgent. So how do you tell them apart?

Fear contracts. Intuition expands. Fear feels tight, small, and closed. It wants you to stay safe, which means stay small, stay stuck, stay where you are. Intuition feels open, even when it's uncomfortable. It might make you nervous, but it also makes you curious. It pulls you forward instead of holding you back.

Fear says "what if it goes wrong?" Intuition says "what if it goes right?" Fear focuses on the worst-case scenario. Intuition focuses on the most aligned path. Fear is loud and repetitive. Intuition is quiet and patient.

When you're not sure which one you're feeling, ask yourself: "Does this feeling make me feel smaller or more alive?" If it's smaller, it's fear. If it's more alive, even if it's scary, it's intuition.

Making Peace with Uncertainty

Here's the truth no one tells you. Using intuition doesn't remove uncertainty. It doesn't guarantee the outcome you want. What it does is guarantee that you made the decision that was most aligned with who you are at that moment. That's all you can ever do.

Life doesn't come with a guarantee. Every decision carries risk. But when you make decisions from intuition, you're making them from your whole self, not just your thinking brain. You're honoring the full range of information your system is giving you. You're trusting that you are capable of handling whatever comes next.

That's the real skill. Not making the "right" decision every time. But making decisions from a place of trust in yourself. Because when you trust yourself, you can handle any outcome. You learn. You adjust. You keep going.

You've been carrying this sensitivity your whole life. You've been told it's a weakness, a problem, something to fix. It's not. It's your most advanced decision-making tool. It's the part of you that sees what your brain misses. It's the part of you that knows before you can explain.

Start using it. Start small. Start today. And watch what happens when you finally trust yourself.

If you're ready to go deeper into developing your intuitive skills, I've put together a free assessment that helps you identify your natural intuitive style. It takes about ten minutes and it'll show you exactly where to focus your practice. Take the assessment here.
